title: Gender — The Foundation Everything Rests On
description: Every German noun is masculine, feminine, or neuter. This isn't arbitrary — there are patterns. Learn the patterns and you'll guess right 70% of the time.

lessons.title: Why Does German Have Genders?
lessons.subtitle: The historical reason — and why it actually helps you.
lessons.hook: English had grammatical gender too — until about 1000 years ago. 'The moon' was masculine in Old English. 'The woman' was neuter. German kept this ancient system. It feels arbitrary at first, but patterns emerge that make it learnable.
lessons.explain: Every German noun belongs to one of three grammatical genders: masculine (der), feminine (die), or neuter (das). The article is like a name tag the noun always wears. You must learn the article WITH the noun — never the noun alone. 'Tisch' (table) is useless. 'der Tisch' is the word. This is the single most important habit to build from day one.

vocabulary.memoryHook: der = blue = masculine. der Mann, der Tisch, der Hund
die = pink = feminine. die Frau, die Schule, die Stadt
das = green = neuter. das Kind, das Buch, das Auto
Things ending in -sch are often masculine
Words ending in -e are VERY often feminine (die Schule, die Küche, die Straße)
Diminutives (-chen, -lein) and many loanwords are neuter

exercises.explanation: In German, the article is inseparable from the noun. Never say 'Tisch' — say 'der Tisch'. The article changes based on the grammatical case, so you must know the base gender to apply the right change later.
Words ending in -e are very frequently feminine (die). Die Schule, die Küche, die Straße, die Blume. This pattern covers roughly 90% of -e ending nouns.

exercises.prompt: Which color represents feminine nouns on Sprich?
exercises.explanation: Our color system: Blue = der (masculine), Pink = die (feminine), Green = das (neuter). These colors are used consistently across every lesson, exercise, and grammar breakdown on the platform.

lessons.title: Gender Patterns — Guess Right 70% of the Time
lessons.subtitle: Endings that reveal gender. Learn these rules and guessing becomes educated.
lessons.hook: The secret every German teacher knows: most textbooks hide the gender patterns. They make students memorize each word individually. We refuse to do that. Here are the patterns that let you predict gender from word endings.
lessons.explain: German word endings reliably predict gender in most cases. This is not 100% reliable — German has exceptions — but these patterns are right more than 70% of the time, which means educated guessing, not random guessing. Learn these once and you'll instantly have a strong prior on any new word you encounter.

vocabulary.memoryHook: RULE: -heit → always die. Freiheit, Gesundheit, Sicherheit, Schönheit
RULE: -ung → always die. Zeitung, Wohnung, Meinung, Hoffnung
RULE: -keit → always die. Möglichkeit, Fähigkeit, Schwierigkeit
RULE: -chen → always das (diminutive suffix). Even though Mädchen means girl — grammatically neuter. Don't fight it, just learn it.
RULE: -chen → das. Brötchen, Mädchen, Häuschen, Kätzchen
RULE: -er (person/profession) → often der. Lehrer, Bäcker, Fahrer, Spieler

exercises.prompt: What article does 'Hoffnung' (hope) take? Hint: look at the ending.
exercises.explanation: Words ending in -ung are ALWAYS feminine (die). This is one of the most reliable rules in German. Hoffnung, Zeitung, Wohnung, Meinung, Übung — all die.

Words ending in -chen are ALWAYS neuter (das). The -chen suffix is a diminutive — it makes things smaller/cuter. And all diminutives in German are neuter, regardless of the base word's gender. Das Kätzchen (from die Katze — the cat).

exercises.prompt: You see a new word: 'Sicherheit' (safety). Without knowing the word, what article do you guess?
exercises.explanation: -heit is always feminine. Sicherheit (safety), Gesundheit (health), Freiheit (freedom), Schönheit (beauty). Every single -heit word is die. This is 100% reliable.
